generated_summary: – "A new line has been crossed," says Rep. Matt Lesser, a Democrat running for a state Senate seat in Connecticut. "Two days after a horrific attack in Pittsburgh, the last thing we expected was to see something like this in Connecticut." Lesser is referring to a mailer sent out by Republican opponent Ed Charamut that shows Lesser, who is Jewish, holding a wad of cash in front of him with a crazed look in his eyes, the Washington Post reports. "The juxtaposition of a Jewish candidate and money in this manner suggests an age-old anti-Semitic trope," says Steve Ginsburg, director of the Connecticut branch of the Anti-Defamation League. Charamut's Jewish campaign treasurer, Allan Greenspan, admits Lesser's eyes were altered to look "bigger and greedy," but he says Lesser's nose was not changed, the Hartford Courant reports. "I assumed it was some sort of mistake or misunderstanding," Lesser says. "I was shocked when I saw the mailer, I've never seen any quite like it." The Courant calls the mailer the "worst kind of anti-Semitism" it has ever seen. "There aren't 'two sides' to anti-Semitism, and there aren't good people

– Greenpeace is taking its campaign against Arctic oil drilling to the skies: Six activists scaled London's tallest skyscraper, the 72-story Shard, last night, the Guardian reports. The climbers, all women, unfurled a banner from the building's roof at around 7pm and were arrested on suspicion of aggravated trespass. "I'm training [the post was written before the climb] for something that I hope will help stop one of the most heartbreaking acts of wanton environmental destruction—drilling for oil in the Arctic," wrote climber Victoria Henry. "I'm over the moon that I can play any part in the mass resistance to this horrifying practice." The Shard is near the three London bases of Shell, which is involved in offshore drilling in the Arctic, Greenpeace said in a statement. "Shell is leading the oil companies' drive into the Arctic, investing billions in its Alaskan and Russian drilling programs," it added. "A worldwide movement of millions has sprung up to stop them, but Shell is refusing to abandon its plans."

– "Robots winning against crime." That's the claim made by a security company whose 300-pound K5 security robot was allegedly assaulted by a drunk man last week in Mountain View, Calif. Police say 41-year-old Jason Sylvain knocked the robot over while it was on patrol in the parking lot of Knightscope, which makes the robots, reports CNET. "The robot did exactly as it was supposed to do—the 'assault' was detected and immediately reported," says a Knightscope VP. "The alarms on the robot sounded, the suspect attempted to flee the scene, and was detained by one of my colleagues and me until the Mountain View Police arrived." The robot suffered some scratches, but it was otherwise unharmed. Sylvain was arrested on charges of prowling and public intoxication and is being held in the Santa Clara County Jail, reports the San Jose Mercury News. Knightscope says it's a "testament to the technology" and the robot is back on patrol.

– Southern California is overdue for a major earthquake, according to a US Geological Survey study that found a 100-mile stretch of the San Andreas Fault is overdue for a quake with a magnitude of 7.5 or greater. The study looked at a section of the fault near the Grapevine in Los Angeles County and found that there have been an average of 10 quakes of that size every 100 years along the fault since 1280, KABC reports. "The stress is building up along the San Andreas, and a large earthquake is inevitable," USGS geophysicist Robert Graves tells the Los Angeles Times. "That would make it difficult to get power to Los Angeles, get supplies in and out of Interstate 5, for example," he says. The last major quake along the fault occurred in 1857, killing more than 200 people and causing billions of dollars in damage. The study found that quakes like the one that occurred in 1857 were more common along the fault in the early 20th century than in the late 19th century, when the fault was most active, the Los Angeles Times reports. Geologists say the area has been under stress for 160 years, and a quake with a magnitude of 7.5 or greater would be devastating for Los Angeles, Ventura, and Kern counties, as well as Southern California.

– The Kellogg Company has become the latest American company to bail on Venezuela, where the economy is so bad that even corn on the cob is hard to find. The cereal-maker announced Tuesday that it was closing its operations in the country, citing "the current economic and social deterioration in the country," the Wall Street Journal reports. According to the BBC, the 400 workers at the Kellogg factory in Maracay had been asked to re-start production, but Venezuela's labor minister says that's illegal. "Kimberly-Clark will continue producing, now in the hands of the workers," Oswaldo Vera says. "We've just turned on the first engine." Kellogg isn't the first company to bail on Venezuela. In 2014, the government took over two plants belonging to US cleaning products maker Clorox after its departure. "This reminds me of the Clorox case," opposition lawmaker Jose Guerra tweeted about Kellogg. "Operations halted due to lack of raw materials, the government took it over, and now it's paralyzed. ... So that means Corn Flakes are over." The government of Nicolas Maduro, who is running for re-election this weekend, says it will take over Kellogg's operations. "I've decided to hand the company over

– Sudan's president, who is wanted by the International Criminal Court on charges of genocide and crimes against humanity in Darfur, slipped out of South Africa today just as a high court there decided to order his arrest, reports CNN. Omar al-Bashir was in the country for a two-day African Union summit, and South Africa's Home Affairs ministry had moved his plane from Johannesburg's Tambo International Airport to a military base south of Pretoria so he wouldn't be caught if he tried to leave the country, reports the New York Times. But the judge who yesterday ordered al-Bashir to stay in the country while the court decided whether he should be arrested warned today that ports of entry and exit weren't following his order, reports Reuters. Al-Bashir is the first head of state to face charges from the ICC, which has accused him of orchestrating genocide and crimes against humanity in Darfur since 2003. Sudan's state news agency reported today that al-Bashir would hold a news conference at the airport after his return.

– One of the most talked-about Super Bowl ads of all time was a Budweiser spot called "Brotherhood." It tells the story of a man who raised a baby Clydesdale and then reunited with him years later. The ad had more than 1.5 million shares as of this writing, mostly on Facebook, making it the third most-shared Super Bowl ad of all time, according to Unruly. Dodge's "9/11" ad is No. 2 with 2.3 million shares, while Budweiser's is No. 24 with 2.3 million shares. Meanwhile, Coca Cola's "Coke Chase" campaign—in which viewers could vote for a team and "sabotage" it by throwing obstacles in their way—had 1.3 million visitors to its website as of yesterday, down from 9 million last year, Mashable reports.

– San Francisco's 58-story Millennium Tower was supposed to be a symbol of the city's tech boom. Instead, residents say it's sinking into the mud and tilting toward its neighbors, CBS News reports. Jerry and Pat Dodson bought a $2.2 million apartment on the Millennium Tower's 42nd floor eight years ago and planned to live out their retirement there. "It's a wonderful location," Pat Dodson tells CBS News. "Everything I had read indicated that it was the best building in San Francisco. It had particularly won awards for construction, which was very important if you're thinking of moving into a high rise." But now the tower is sinking 17 inches and tilting 14 inches to the northwest, according to data from engineers. Homeowners are suing the tower's developer, Millennium Partners, and city officials are holding hearings into what's causing the problem. "Nobody has owned up to why this building is not performing," says Aaron Peskin, a city supervisor who's leading the hearings. The tower was built on a foundation made of 645 feet of reinforced concrete wrapped in glass. It was supposed to be the tallest residential building west of the Mississippi at the time of its construction. According to the San Francisco Chronicle, the tower was

– Abdul Qadeer Khan, the founder of Pakistan's nuclear weapons program, says North Korea bribed Pakistani military officials to gain access to nuclear technology in the late 1990s. Khan has released a letter he says is a North Korean official's 1998 letter to him, in which the official explains the $3 million payment and gifts, including jewelry, were made to two Pakistani military officials, the Washington Post reports. Pakistani officials, including those named as recipients of the cash, have called the letter a fake. Khan, whom some in his country have hailed as a national hero, is at odds with many Pakistani officials, who have said he acted alone in selling nuclear secrets. Nevertheless, if the letter is genuine, it would reveal a remarkable instance of corruption related to nuclear weapons. US officials said they had not seen the letter before, and that government experts concluded after examining a copy that the signature appears authentic. Khan also has released what he says is a copy of a North Korean official's 1998 letter to him, written in English, that spells out details of the clandestine deal. Some Western intelligence officials and other experts have said they think the letter is authentic and that it offers confirmation of a transaction they have long suspected but could never prove.

– The US is dead last out of 11 industrialized countries when it comes to health care, according to a new report from the Commonwealth Fund think tank. The US was last in terms of efficiency, equity, access, and quality, the Atlantic reports. The UK ranked first, Switzerland second, and Norway third. The US also came in last in per capita health care costs, at $8,508, while Norway came in second at $5,669. "Although the US spends more on health care than any other country and has the highest proportion of specialist physicians, survey findings indicate that from the patients’ perspective, and based on outcome indicators, the performance of American health care is severely lacking," the report concludes. The US also came in last in preventive care and healthy life expectancy, the Atlantic notes. The report looked at 2004 to 2014, the same time that the Affordable Care Act came into full effect. The data for the report was collected before the full implementation of ObamaCare, so it's possible that the US may rise in future rankings, Vox reports. But the study's lead author says that improvement "is a matter of accountability, having information on your performance relative to your peers, and being held accountable to achieving a kind of care that patients should expect to get

– A Nashville father on his way to the airport left his 1-year-old daughter in his pickup truck while he went on a business trip, and she died as a result, police say. Matt Barker left his home Wednesday morning with his two children, a 5-year-old and a 1-year-old, to drop them off at a day care center a few miles away, reports the Tennessean. He returned about half an hour later, via a ride-share service, and then flew out of Nashville International Airport, police say. His wife, Jenny Barker, left for her job in another vehicle thinking that both children were at the day care center, police say. She went to the center in the evening to pick up Katera Barker and the child's 5-year-old sibling and was told that Katera never had been dropped off. Jenny Barker then telephoned her husband and came to the realization that Katera, whom the couple had adopted recently, was still in the pickup truck. She pulled her into her driveway at 5:42pm and began CPR until paramedics arrived, reports USA Today. Katera was pronounced dead at a hospital. Wednesday's high was 89 degrees, so the temperature inside the truck could have reached nearly 120 degrees. No charges

– A passenger caused a scare on a Hong Kong-Newark flight today by screaming that he had information on NSA leaker Edward Snowden and wanted the plane to be diverted to Canada, the AP reports. Passengers tackled the man, who was reportedly in his 30s, and handcuffed him, and he was taken to a hospital for observation. "He was convinced he was going to die before this flight landed," a fellow passenger tells ABC News. "He was screaming stuff about national security advisers, the CIA, saying names of people [he claimed were] working for the CIA," the passenger says. "He was saying that we couldn't land the plane, we had to divert the plane. He couldn't land in the US." Another passenger says the man was afraid of being poisoned by a fellow passenger. "He saw everybody as a threat," the passenger says. "He thought everyone was working for the FBI, the CIA. ... He was drawing a parallel between [National Security Agency leaker] Edward Snowden and himself. ... He was convinced he was going to die before this flight landed."

– With less than a month to go until Election Day, the New York Times reports that Donald Trump's support is at an all-time low and he could be forced to drop out of the race if he doesn't turn things around on Sunday in his second debate with Hillary Clinton. Republican sources tell the Times that congressional candidates in states such as Florida, North Carolina, and Pennsylvania, where Trump is behind in the polls, may decide to leave the race if Trump does badly in Sunday's debate. A round-up of coverage: Politico reports that Trump is "blaming a trail across the West" and that his chances of winning the election are not much different from Mitt Romney's in 2012. "The path to 270 goes through all the same states as it did in 2012 and 2008," says Katie Packer, Romney's deputy campaign manager in 2012. "Michigan, Wisconsin, and Pennsylvania aren't really even swing states. The math still comes down to suburban women and Latino voters." The Washington Post reports that Trump's unpopularity could cost the GOP dearly in states such as Pennsylvania, Michigan, and Ohio, where recent polls show that Clinton has opened a lead that Trump may not be able to overcome. The Los Angeles Times reports that Trump is

– If your password is tough to remember, scientists may be able to replace it with your own brainwaves. Researchers at Binghamton University read volunteers a list of 75 acronyms and recorded their brain responses to them, according to a post at Eureka Alert. The volunteers' brainwaves were different enough to allow a computer to identify them with 94% accuracy. "If someone's fingerprint is stolen, that person can't just grow a new finger to replace the compromised fingerprint—the fingerprint for that person is compromised forever," says a co-author of the study in Neurocomputing. "Fingerprints are 'non-cancellable.' Brainprints, on the other hand, are potentially cancellable. So, in the unlikely event that attackers were actually able to steal a brainprint from an authorized user, the authorized user could then 'reset' their brainprint." The researchers don't see brainprints as a replacement for passwords just yet, but they do see the technology having potential in high security environments. "We tend to see the applications of this system as being more along the lines of high-security physical locations, like the Pentagon or Air Force Labs, where there aren't that many users that are authorized to enter, and those users don't need to

– Police in Ferguson, Mo., have released surveillance video of Michael Brown robbing a convenience store on the day he was shot dead, but they say it doesn't show the shooting itself, CNN reports. Police Chief Thomas Jackson says the "robbery does not relate to the initial contact between the officer and Michael Brown," and Wilson approached Brown because he was "walking down the middle of the street blocking traffic," reports the St. Louis Post-Dispatch. (See the video here.) But Brown's friends say he didn't appear to be stealing anything at the time. "He was just walking around the store," one tells the Post-Dispatch. "He didn't seem to be doing anything. He was just walking around the store." Another says Brown was trying to surrender when Wilson approached him. "I don't think he had his hands in the air," the friend says. "I don't think he had his gun. I don't think he had his Taser. I don't think he had his hands in the air." Police also released the name of the officer who shot Brown: Darren Wilson. The 28-year-old, who is on paid administrative leave, has been with the Ferguson force for six years

– The Nazis tore down their infamous Treblinka death camp in 1943, but the site was left largely unexplored—until now. Archaeologists working at the site in Poland have uncovered several previously unknown mass graves, as well as evidence that the camp once held gas chambers, reports LiveScience. "Treblinka: Hitler's Killing Machine," which airs Saturday on the Smithsonian Channel, documents the "non-invasive" archaeological work done at the site by forensic archaeologist Caroline Sturdy Colls. "These artifacts are there, and these human remains are on the surface, and they're not being recorded or recovered," says Colls, whose team used lidar, a laser technology that allows researchers to scan the ground with lasers. "What that revealed was the presence of previously unknown mass graves," Colls says. "The largest of the mass graves as revealed on lidar was 63 feet by 58 feet in size (19.2 by 17.6 meters). Indeed, when the archaeology team began digging to confirm the lidar results, they uncovered shoes, ammunition, and bones—including bones with cut marks indicating that the victims had been stabbed or otherwise assaulted. After digging three small test trenches to confirm each mass grave, Colls and her team reburied

– The CIA's conclusion that Russia worked to get Donald Trump elected is both "stunning and not surprising," the next leader of the Senate Democrats says. In a statement issued Saturday, Sen. Charles Schumer said the idea that any country "could be meddling in our elections should shake both political parties to their core," the Washington Post reports. "Senate Democrats will join with our Republican colleagues next year to demand a congressional investigation and hearings to get to the bottom of this," Schumer said. "It's imperative that our intelligence community turns over any relevant information so that Congress can conduct a full investigation." According to the Post's sources, CIA officials told senators it is now "quite clear" Russia was trying to help Trump win the election by hacking the Democratic National Committee and releasing stolen emails. The CIA's conclusion was shared with senators in a closed-door briefing last week. "It is the assessment of the intelligence community that Russia's goal here was to favor one candidate over the other, to help Trump get elected," a senior US official briefed on the intelligence presentation told senators. "That's the consensus view." According to the Post's sources, intelligence agencies believe the same Russians who broke into the DNC and the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee also

– A preschool in Hawaii is taking a closer look at its snack policy after three children took sips of Pine-Sol, the AP reports. Officials say a classroom assistant at a Honolulu preschool mistook the household cleaner for apple juice. Paramedics were called to the Kilohana United Methodist Church Preschool to evaluate three girls, ages 4 and 5, on Tuesday morning. Paramedics found no signs of trauma or sickness, and the girls' parents declined to take them to the hospital. The preschool says only sips were taken before the lead teacher realized the liquid was not apple juice. The assistant picked up the container of yellow-brown liquid off a cleanup cart in the kitchen and poured it into cups. The cleaner was its original container and properly labeled. Inspectors say the assistant no longer works at the school. While the two products are similar in color, they smell distinctly different. "I think it's extremely terrifying. It's very, very scary, but it's hard for me or any of the people that I've spoken to understand how it happened in the first place," says parent Turina Lovelin. Parents say the school sent an email out to let them know what happened, and a meeting was also held Thursday night to answer their

– Toronto billionaires Barry and Honey Sherman were "in fact targeted" when they were found dead in their home on Dec. 15, police say. "We believe now, through six weeks' work and review, that we have sufficient evidence to describe this as a double homicide investigation and that both Honey and Barry Sherman were in fact targeted," Det.-Sgt. Susan Gomes told reporters Friday, per the Toronto Star. The couple, 75 and 70, were found hanging by belts from a poolside railing in their Toronto home, and police say they have an "extensive list of people that we're looking forward to speaking to." The list includes people who had access to the home through a realtor's "lock box," Gomes said. The home was on the market, and the realtor removed the listing five days after the bodies of the Shermans were found, the CBC reports. Police say they've obtained or executed 20 judicial authorizations and searched more than 150 pieces of evidence, including the computers of Barry Sherman's pharmaceutical company, Apotex. They've also obtained 2,000 hours of surveillance footage from nearby businesses. "Legal complexities and some executions (of warrants) have been challenging, given the litigious nature of Barry Sherman's businesses," Gomes

– Donald Trump says he'll skip Thursday night's Fox News GOP debate because Fox News is treating him "unfairly," and his campaign manager says the real reason is money. "He will not be participating in the Fox News debate on Thursday night," Corey Lewandowski told reporters in Iowa on Tuesday, per Reuters. "When they sent out the wise-guy press releases a little while ago, done by some PR person, along with Roger Ailes, I said: 'Bye, bye.'" Trump's campaign manager added that the campaign is moving ahead with plans to hold a rally in Iowa for the Wounded Warriors and that the money raised will go to the charity. In a statement, Fox said it had "warned" Lewandowski "not to level any more threats" against Megyn Kelly. "We learned from a secret back channel that the Ayatollah and Putin both intend to treat Donald Trump unfairly when they meet with him if he becomes president," the network said, adding that "capitulating to politicians' ultimatums about a debate moderator violates all journalistic standards, as do threats." Ted Cruz was one of the first to react to Trump's decision to skip the debate, the New York Times reports. "This is a job interview

– Rescue crews using bulldozers and their bare hands raced to dig out survivors from a strong earthquake that reduced three central Italian towns to rubble early Wednesday. The death toll stood at 120 but the number of dead and missing was uncertain given the huge number of vacationers in the area for summer's final days, reports the AP. Residents wakened before dawn by the temblor emerged from their crumbled homes to find what they described as apocalyptic scenes "like Dante's Inferno," with entire blocks of buildings turned into piles of sand and rock, thick dust choking the air and a putrid smell of gas. "The town isn't here anymore," said Sergio Pirozzi, the mayor of the hardest-hit town, Amatrice. "I believe the toll will rise." The magnitude 6 quake struck at 3:36am and was felt across a broad swath of central Italy, including Rome, where residents woke to a long swaying followed by aftershocks. The temblor shook the Lazio region and Umbria and Le Marche on the Adriatic coast. Premier Matteo Renzi visited the zone Wednesday, greeted rescue teams and survivors and announced the toll stood at 120. He promised the quake-prone area that "No family, no city, no hamlet will be

– Republicans have reason to be optimistic heading into the midterm elections, writes James Carville at the Hill. Republicans need to pick up six seats to take control of the Senate, and Montana, South Dakota, and West Virginia are all seen as gimmes for the GOP. They'll need to flip two more, and they've got plenty of options. But they also have to win in blue and purple states that Barack Obama has been winning, and that's a tall order. Democrats, meanwhile, have reason to be optimistic. A new poll by the Washington Post and ABC News shows that the Democratic Party is viewed favorably by 49% of respondents, while the Republican Party is viewed favorably by 35%. "In essence, in what other endeavor would you be selling something attached to a brand so unpopular and expect success?" asks Carville. "So many people conclude that how people view a political party has nothing to do with their vote." Democrats, he writes, tend to "respect and value expertise" and shouldn't be "placed at their consensus out of anything other than a high degree of professionalism."

– Scotland Yard detectives will soon be heading to the Portugal resort where Madeleine McCann disappeared seven years ago. Excavations are planned at three sites near the Praia da Luz resort, a source tells the Daily Mirror. A forensic team will use radar equipment to search for clues underground, the source says. "It will be a heartbreaking and hugely emotional time for Madeleine’s poor parents," the source says. "Kate and Gerry have been told police will be conducting the searches in and around Praia da Luz as soon as they get the green light from Portuguese authorities." Police have "assured Kate and Gerry that it does not mean they are specifically searching for her body," the source says. "They are doing searches as much as to rule scenarios out as much as rule them in." A Metropolitan Police spokesman wouldn't confirm or deny the report, the Guardian reports. "We are not going to give a running commentary," he said. "From the beginning of Operation Grange, we have made it clear that speculation has been unhelpful to the investigation and that continues to be the case. We will not be commenting on any speculation surrounding this investigation."

– Seth Rogen's mom tweeted Wednesday, "Falling asleep after sex is like shavasana after yoga.!"—and it got Seth's mom, Sandy, into a whole lot of hot water. Mashable reports it all started when Sandy tweeted to her more than 41,000 followers, "Dearest @Sethrogen, falling asleep after sex is like Jesus f---ing Christ mom." The tweet was retweeted thousands of times, and when Seth replied, "Jesus f---ing Christ mom," his mom tweeted back, "Whatever!! It's just an observation of life !" That's when things got a little heated. "Whateverrrrrrrr mom mm!" Sandy tweeted, to which Seth replied, "Whateverrrrrrrr mom mm!" His dad, Dan Rogen, chimed in, "It's gnarly," to which Sandy replied, "Lame!" But it wasn't the only family conversation that went down Wednesday. " moments like this are why Twitter was invented," People quotes Sandy as saying. (Seth's latest movie is out in August.)

– In his new book, Unintended Consequences, former Bain Capital executive Peter Conard argues that the financial crisis wasn't caused by "corrupt bankers selling dodgy financial products," but by a simple old-fashioned run on the banks. He argues that the banks were just doing their job by turning short-term assets into long-term loans that helped the economy grow. "Depositors ran to the bank to withdraw their money only to discover, like the citizens of Bedford Falls," referring to the movie It's a Wonderful Life, "that there was no money in the vault," Conard tells David Brooks in the New York Times. "All that money had been lent." Conard argues that the banks didn't cause the crisis, as many others have argued, but that withdrawals from pension funds, insurance companies, and other institutional investors did, five times more than losses from mortgage-backed securities. "The banks did what we wanted them to do," he says. "They put short-term money back into the economy. What they didn't expect is that depositors would withdraw their money en masse since 1929." Conard, who worked for Mitt Romney's campaign before leaving for Bain, says he hopes his book will help readers understand why Romney

– If you're in Denver, there's a good chance you now own some pot. Commercial real estate firm CBRE says one in 11 industrial buildings in the city is now home to marijuana grow facilities, the Denver Post reports. "Right now, we feel like the marijuana grow market, we call it overgrown," says Paul Kluck, first vice president of CBRE Industrial Services in Denver. "That 3.7 million square feet, some of it's still vacant and there are some growers who haven't reached full capacity. We don't feel like there's additional demand for grow facilities other than the normal in-and-out in existing facilities." Between 2009 and 2014, pot businesses accounted for more than a third of all industrial space leased in the city, according to CBRE. The industry's surge into the real estate market has led to near-record high rents and low vacancy rates, the Post reports. Average industrial lease rates in the third quarter were $7.05 per square foot across the metro area, up 5% year over year and approaching record highs not seen since 2004. For lower-quality warehouse space, known in the trade as Class B and C, lease rates jumped 56% in the past five years to $6.34 per square foot in key

– Two estranged sisters return to their childhood home to throw a party, only to discover it's more than they bargained for. Here's what critics are saying: "Sisters is strenuously vulgar, as well as studiously raunchy, and both of these eminently likable comedians want us to know that the whole silly, chaotic thing is in good fun," writes Manohla Dargis at the New York Times. "But come on, people, it's really bad fun that's badly directed (by Jason Moore), badly written (by Paula Pell), badly performed and bad news for anyone who cares about the quality of big-screen comedy." Fey and Poehler "fall easily into sync with Kate and Maura's slightly competitive but largely affectionate dynamic, with Kate egging on her good-girl sibling to let loose and live a little, even if that means that, for the Last Great Kegger of All Time, at least, it's Kate who will have to be the designated Party Mom." It's "a bacchanal of epic and increasingly hilarious proportions," writes Ty Burr at the Boston Globe. "What starts as a dull grown-up affair of people sharing about menopause, vaginal rejuvenation and the sundry indignities of

– A Florida developer's plan to build townhouses on a 3-acre plot of land that once housed a black cemetery is causing a stir—because where are the bodies? The Deerfield Beach City Commission will hold a special meeting Tuesday to discuss the development, the Sun-Sentinel reports. "There are no bodies there," says Mayor Jean Robb. "It's been undeveloped for years. It will add to the tax rolls. Why leave it empty?" The site was once referred to as the "old colored cemetery" and as many as 400 bodies are believed to have been buried there, the Sun-Sentinel notes. The first plans to build on the site came in 1974, but angry residents quashed the move. According to one city study, the leader of the protest movement said his wife was buried there. In 1974, he exhumed her body and had it reburied elsewhere. Mayor Jean Robb said she thinks the bodies were moved to the city-owned Pineview Cemetery near Martin Luther King Jr. Avenue but has been unable to confirm that hunch. The city clerk even reviewed old documents stored in a warehouse. City officials, however, point to two studies, in 1986 and 2005, where experts determined there are no bodies at the site. Both studies

– The Navy is investigating the death of a SEAL trainee who lost consciousness during a training exercise Friday in California, the Virginian-Pilot reports. James Derek Lovelace, 21, was in the last day of basic underwater demolition/SEAL training at Naval Amphibious Base Coronado when he passed out in a pool. "It's a physiological test to see if a guy can do exercises in the water," an official tells NBC News. Lovelace was wearing a camouflage utility uniform and diving masks when he passed out. He was pulled from the pool and taken to a hospital, where he was pronounced dead. The training exercise Lovelace was taking part in is considered one of the most difficult in the military. It involves treading water and swimming in a pool while wearing diving masks and a camouflage utility uniform. According to the Washington Post, Lovelace joined the Navy in November. He graduated from the Navy's boot camp in Great Lakes, Ill., in January. An investigation is underway.

– An Army wife is suing the government after she says an Army nurse botched her childbirth operation, causing serious damage to her reproductive and digestive system, the Army Times reports. Amy Herbst says the nurse at Blanchfield Army Community Hospital at Fort Campbell, Ky., performed an episiotomy during the second stage of her labor without her consent. The incision is made in the perineum—the area between the vagina and anus—to help deliver a baby that is in distress or to prevent muscles from tearing. After the procedure, Herbst says she "began to experience fecal urgency and incontinence, including periodic leaking of stool and excessive flatulence." Herbst says the nurse-midwife repaired the episiotomy with sutures, but after Herbst left the hospital, she "began to experience fecal urgency and incontinence, including periodic leaking of stool and excessive flatulence," the suit states. Herbst says she can't continue working as a professional opera singer because of the complications of the procedure. "She is suffering though a very embarrassing and very significant injury, and frankly, the prognosis of a fully successful repair is pretty low," her attorney tells the Army Times.

– For the first time, the White House has acknowledged that the US has been taking "direct action" against members of al-Qaeda in Yemen and Somalia, reports the AP. The revelation comes in the White House's semiannual report to Congress on US combat operations abroad. The report doesn't provide details of any military operations in either country, but it does acknowledge that "direct action" includes attacks by armed drones. "In all cases we are focused on those al-Qaeda members and affiliates who pose a direct threat to the United States and to our national interests," said Pentagon press secretary George Little after the report's release. "This report contains information about these operations owing to their growing significance in our overall counterterrorism effort." Under War Powers Resolution of 1973, the White House is required to report to Congress every six months on US combat operations abroad. The last report, on Dec. 15, 2011, made no mention of Yemen or Somalia. Instead it said that in relation to efforts against al-Qaeda, the US was working with partners "with a particular focus on countries within the US Central Command's area of responsibility." "Our joint efforts have resulted in direct action against a limited number of AQAP operatives and senior leaders in that country who posed a terrorist

– Mitt Romney's Facebook page is losing "likes" at a dizzying rate, CNN reports. The page was losing 593 likes an hour Friday, and by Saturday it was up to 847 likes an hour. As of this morning, Romney's page was losing around 11 likes per minute, according to DisappearingRomney.com, a website that tracks the number of people who "unliked" Romney's page. Romney's Facebook and Twitter accounts went quiet after the election, but he's still got more than 12 million fans on Facebook and 1.7 million followers on Twitter. Paul Ryan hasn't posted to social media since the election, but he did have his personal Facebook and Twitter accounts updated over the weekend.

– "It would be scary for anyone," an Arlington, Va., tenant tells NBC Washington. She was right. The woman says she heard footsteps above her Friday night and checked in with her landlord, who used the storage above her home's attic. Around 1:30am, police arrived and searched the attic, where they found 60-year-old homeless man Anthony Jones. "As we [officers] were coming up that staircase, they announced that officers were on scene and that if you were in the attic, to come forward," a police spokeswoman says. Jones came forward and was arrested and charged with unlawful entry. Police found his backpack, clothes, and bedding in the attic, and he's being held without bail. It's not clear how long Jones had been living in the house, but "it would be scary for anyone," the tenant says.

– Catherine Zeta-Jones says she and husband Michael Douglas "are taking some time apart to evaluate and work on their marriage," reports AP. They haven't been photographed together since April, and People says they've decided to take a break. "The stress has taken a toll on their marriage," says a friend. "They want the best for their kids, no matter what happens." Douglas, 68, and Zeta-Jones, 43, have two kids together.

– Chen Guangcheng has left the US embassy and is under the care of Chinese authorities, but he's not done with his standoff with Beijing. The dissident tells Reuters that he wants the government to punish officials he accuses of imprisoning him on false charges and abusing him during 19 months of extra-judicial house arrest. "I think that at least (continuing) rights defense would be very natural," he says. "Like when someone hits you, don't you flinch? I think that defending our rights is also a basic natural response." Chen says he has spoken to a Chinese government official who has promised to investigate his accusations. "That's the promise, but for now it's just a promise," he says. "There hasn't been any concrete action yet." Chen, who has an intestinal problem and a broken foot, says he wants to study in the US, but it's not clear when he will be allowed to do so.

– In 1963, Barbara Bezant and Lyn Jeffries went to see the Beatles at the Lewisham Odeon in London and recorded a message to Paul McCartney. They sent the reel-to-reel tape to the Finsbury Park Astoria, where the Beatles were playing at the time. Decades later, the women, who were 17 and 19 at the time, received a letter from Sir Paul McCartney thanking them for their "lovely tape" and saying "better late than never," the BBC reports. The women, who were living in Essex at the time, hadn't seen each other for 40 years, but they were reunited with the letter on the UK's One Show. "It was like sitting in a bubble and being there when these two girls were making this tape," says local historian David McDermott, who listened to the tape. "The hairs on the back of my neck stood up and it made me wonder if these two were still with us." McDermott bought the tape at a flea market decades later, and it was played on the show. "This dream is just to come round the back and see you, but I don't suppose that'll ever happen," the message on the tape said. "But we can always live in hope, can't we?"

– A married Christian couple were beaten to death by a mob in Pakistan today after being accused of desecrating the Koran, police say. The victims, identified only as Shama and Shehzad, were working at the brick kiln where they lived in the town of Kot Radha Kishan near Lahore, reports AFP. "A mob attacked a Christian couple after accusing them of desecration of the Holy Quran and later burnt their bodies at a brick kiln where they worked," a police official tells AFP. "Yesterday an incident of desecration of the Holy Quran took place in the area and today the mob first beat the couple and later set their bodies on fire at a brick kiln." A Christian rights activist tells NBC News that workers at the kiln discovered that the couple had set fire to verses of the Koran after their daughter burned items left behind by her father, who died last year. Police say up to 35 people were involved in the attack and arrests have been made.

– The Trump administration is looking to keep the US in the Paris climate change agreement, three months after President Trump said the US would pull out of the deal if it didn't find more favorable terms, the Wall Street Journal reports. According to the New York Times, the Trump administration is considering staying in the Paris agreement to fight climate change "under the right conditions," offering to re-engage in the international deal three months after Trump said the US would pull out if it didn't find more favorable terms. The Trump administration's offer was made during a meeting Saturday of more than 30 ministers led by Canada, China, and the European Union in Montreal. During the meeting, US officials discussed revising US climate-change goals, two participants said, signaling a compromise. "The US will remain in the Paris accord under the right conditions," the White House said in a statement Saturday. "The United States will not withdraw from the Paris accord, but we will re-engage in it under the right conditions."

– Stormy Daniels' defamation suit against President Trump was dismissed Monday—and Trump is now threatening to go after her "horseface." "Great, now I can go after Horseface and her 3rd rate lawyer in the Great State of Texas," the president tweeted Tuesday. "She knows nothing about me, a total con!" Daniels, whose real name is Stephanie Clifford, fired back on Twitter, saying Trump's comments "demonstrated his incompetence, hatred of women, and lack of self control." She added: "Game on, Tiny." The timing of Trump's tweet "made it tougher for Republican candidates to attract suburban women and it makes it tougher for women to believe their president respects them," a former White House official tells Politico. "It's not going to impact his base—stuff like this never does—but it makes it tougher for Republican candidates to attract suburban women and it makes it tougher for women to believe their president respects them." (Daniels says she slept with Trump shortly after his wife gave birth.)

– A batch of letters from John F. Kennedy to Swedish socialite Gunilla von Post sold for $15,000 at Upper East Side auction house Doyle New York yesterday—a far cry from the $115,000 his previous letters to von Post sold for in 2010, reports ABC News. Kennedy and von Post met in Cannes, France, in the summer of 1953, and the letters date from June 1954 to August 1956. They were found in von Post's belongings after her 2011 death. In the letters, Kennedy asks the 21-year-old beauty to meet him in Stockholm, tries to persuade her to marry him in Sweden, and tells her she "looked well and happy" in a photo she sent. "I had a wonderful time last summer with you," he wrote. "It is a bright memory of my life—you are wonderful and I miss you. Jack." In her 1997 book, Love, Jack, von Post writes that the two spent a week consummating their love all over the country and that Kennedy tried to end his marriage with Jackie and bring von Post to America. Ultimately, too many obstacles stood in their way—his political ambitions, his father, and Kennedy's and von Post's "mutual sensitivity" to Jackie's miscarriage in 1955 and

The former governor of Massachusetts will focus on the "state of the race" in his speech at the Hinckley Institute of Politics Student Forum at the University of Utah and won't endorse any of his rivals, sources say, but he'll likely cite Trump's failure to release his tax returns as one of the reasons he doesn't think the New York businessman is the right person to lead the party. "Donald Trump has shown an extraordinary ability to understand how our economy works and to create jobs, he has done it in Nevada and across the country. He is one of the few people who stood up and said China has taken jobs from Americans," Romney said in a Facebook post Wednesday. "We believe in free trade but we have to have a President to stand up and not allow people to cheat day in and day out." Romney has previously slammed Trump for failing to release his tax returns and for "collusion of repugnant bigotry," but he's not expected to go so far as to call Trump a loser, notes FiveThirtyEight, which predicts that

– It's a girl for Serena Williams and Reddit co-founder Alexis Ohanian, E! Online reports. The 35-year-old tennis star gave birth to a baby girl at a hospital in Palm Beach, Fla., on Friday. "Tennis star Serena Williams gives birth to a baby girl weighing 6 pounds, 13 ounces. Mom and baby doing well," E! quotes a WPBF reporter as tweeting. The couple got engaged in December. Williams took maternity leave from tennis in April but says she plans to return to the sport after the baby is born.

That's the intro to a Craigslist ad for a 1999 Toyota Corolla, the Mirror reports. "Let me tell you a story. One day my Corolla started making a strange sound. I didn't give a s--- and ignored it. – "This isn't amateur hour," Det. Charles Geiger of the Los Angeles Police Department's Robbery-Homicide Division tells NBC Los Angeles. "The next level is people get tied up, maybe somebody gets hurt." Police say three men known as the "Cold Hand Bandits" have robbed 23 banks in the Los Angeles area since November and are still on the loose, CBS Los Angeles reports. Police say the men typically walk into a bank, hand a teller a note, and demand money. "They claim to be armed while threatening violence if their demands are not met," the LAPD says in a statement. Police say the Cold Hand Bandits have jumped over the teller's counter in at least two robberies and jumped into another when they didn't get what they wanted. The FBI is working with police to track down the suspects, who are believed to be male blacks between the ages of 25 and 30. Police say the Cold Hand Bandits have robbed at least five other banks. "They're hitting all over the place," Det. Joe Harris with the LAPD's Robbery-Homicide Division tells NBC. "It is scary," the manager of a bank where the Cold Hand Bandits have robbed twice tells NBC. "

– The death toll from Superstorm Sandy has risen to at least 46 in the US and Puerto Rico, reports NBC News. That includes 18 in New York City, reports the New York Times. The total in the US is expected to be closer to 50, reports the Wall Street Journal, including at least 10 in New York and six in New Jersey. In the Caribbean, Sandy was blamed for at least 68 deaths. Here's what else you need to know: Power: More than 6.6 million homes and businesses are without power in New York and New Jersey, two-thirds of them in New York and New Jersey. Flights: Sandy has forced the cancellation of 18,000 flights so far, and that number is expected to rise. Subways: New York City's subway will likely be out for four to five days, reports NBC News. Gov. Andrew Cuomo says the South Ferry subway station was "flooded up to the ceiling," and each tube of the Brooklyn-Battery Tunnel was filled with 43 million gallons of water. Schools and government offices: Schools and government offices are closed in 20 states and DC, including all of New York City, reports the Times. Click for more on Sandy's aftermath.

– Cows are notorious methane gas producers, belching as much as 264 gallons of gas produced by fermentation each day, Penn State College of Agricultural Sciences reports. And that's a big problem, according to the EPA. Cows and other ruminant animals, including sheep and goats, produce 26% of the country's total methane emissions, which is a greenhouse gas with more short-term warming consequences than carbon dioxide. Now, a Dutch life sciences and materials company is reporting in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences that it has developed a powder that can be added to cow feed to produce a 30% reduction in methane emissions. The powder, which is called a "methane inhibitor," blocks one of the steps of the enzymatic process that produces methane from carbon dioxide and hydrogen, reports Scientific American. It also appears to help cows gain more body weight, which is good news for both the cow and the planet. DSM, which is partially funding the research, says the powder can produce "a reduction of over 30% in methane emissions with no negative impact on animal welfare, performance, or the amount of feed they consume." The company's "Clean Cow" project has been working on this solution for years, and the new study is the first to show that

– Steve Bannon isn't backing down from Roy Moore. The embattled Alabama Senate candidate was back on the campaign trail in Fairhope on Tuesday night, and the former White House strategist didn't hold back. "Judge Roy Moore has more honor and integrity in his pinky finger than your entire family," Bannon told a rally, per Vox. "You hid behind your religion. You went to France to be a missionary while men were dying in Vietnam. Do not talk about honor and integrity. You ran for commander in chief and had five sons—not one day of service in Afghanistan or Iraq. We have 7,000 dead and 52,000 casualties, and where were the Romneys during those wars?" Bannon went on to compare Moore to another high-profile Republican opponent: Mitt Romney. Romney, who received a deferment while serving in Vietnam, tweeted Monday that Moore "would be a stain on the GOP and on the nation" if elected. Bannon's attack on Romney appears to be part of his strategy to keep the 2012 presidential nominee from running for the Senate in Utah next year, though it's not clear whether he'll actually be able to do that, notes FiveThirtyEight. Bannon has previously said he plans to "send shockwaves" to political and media elites

– John Boehner's two-step strategy to punish President Obama over immigration has taken a big step forward. The House today approved a bill that would prohibit the White House from protecting millions of undocumented immigrants from deportation, reports the Washington Post. The vote was 219-197, with 18 Republicans joining Democrats in voting against it. The vote comes ahead of a Dec. 11 deadline to pass a spending bill and avoid a government shutdown. Boehner wants to use the spending bill to try to undo Obama's executive actions on immigration, but the White House has threatened a veto, and the Senate hasn't even taken up the House bill yet. "The American people elected us to heed their will and not to bow to the whims of a White House that regards the legislative process established by the Constitution as little more than a nuisance," Boehner said before the vote, reports the Los Angeles Times. "To do anything less would be an act of monumental arrogance." The White House, however, says the bill is legal and stands no chance in the Senate, reports AP. In the Senate, meanwhile, Democrats are working on a bill of their own to block Obama's executive actions.

– Pablo Neruda's publisher says it has found more than 20 unpublished poems among the Nobel Prize-winning Chilean poet's papers that are of "extraordinary quality," the Guardian reports. It's "the biggest find in Spanish literature in recent years," says Neruda's Spanish publisher, Seix Barral. The poems, which range from love poetry to poems dealing with everyday objects, were written by the mature Neruda, says the publisher, after 1950's Canto General. They will be published in Latin America and early 2015 in Spain. The poems were found in boxes of the poet's manuscripts kept at the Pablo Neruda foundation in Chile, and they will be published in late 2014 in Latin America and early 2015 in Spain. Adam Feinstein, author of the acclaimed biography Pablo Neruda: A Passion for Life, called the lines "remarkable" and said that it appears the newly-found poems "are full of Neruda's richly imaginative use of language and imagery." "This is an enormously exciting discovery," Feinstein tells the Guardian. "In his lifetime, Neruda used to joke that one day they would go as far as to publish his socks. Now they have uncovered a whole new batch of his poems which lay un

Here's what you need to know about Trump's visit to Poland, which the country's right-wing ruling party sees as a "diplomatic coup": The choice of Krasinski Square: It was at the heart of Poland's 1944 failed uprising against Nazi German occupation and its location could be interpreted as an attempt by Trump to publicly remind Germany of its dark past, reports the BBC. "They have the same view of the world," says the mayor of a Polish town who has come under pressure from the government for attempting to resettle refugees. "Our government thinks that Poland has to rise, to be great again, the same as Trump." The visit is controversial: "It's a controversial visit," says Magorzata Bonikowska of the Warsaw-based think tank Center for International Relations. "It could be read as a signal that the US feels closer to Poland than to its EU partners. Poland, you're OK Trump often criticizes NATO members for failing to meet the military alliance's 2% of GDP defense spending goal. In Poland, he won't

"Now I can say that I still haven't had an abortion, but I wish I had," the Girls creator said on her podcast, Women of the Hour, last week. "I think it's important to normalize abortion," she continued. "I think it's important to not stigmatize it. I think it's important to not look at it as something that's going to be a death sentence for you, and instead look at it as a life-saving procedure that should be available to women all over the world." Jezebel calls Dunham's comments "free red meat for the sort of troll who believes pro-choice feminists spend their days praying to their lord and savior Margaret Sanger that they'll get accidentally impregnated so they can have one of those abortions the gals at the nail salon can't stop gabbing about." – Thirteen babies in rural areas of France have been born missing arms, forearms, or hands since 2007, and public health officials there initially said the cases were "probably down to chance," the Guardian reports. But doctors at a research organization now say the likelihood of it being coincidental is "more than infinitesimal" and they want a full investigation into the cases. "These malformations are very rare, but also very specific," epidemiologist Emmanuelle Amar says. "There is something, some product, that is cutting the limbs at the time the embryo is developing. We must search for it." She says the most likely cause is "environmental or medical," but she warns against making presumptions. "These are future children, future generations we are talking about, but they are also individual human dramas," she says. The malformations were first reported near the village of Druillat in the Ain department of eastern France, where seven babies were born without arms, forearms, or hands between 2009 and 2014. All lived within a 10.5-mile radius of the village in an area where maize or sunflowers were cultivated. Three years later, three children were reported to have been born with similar birth defects between 2007 and 2008 in the town of Mouzeil in the Loire-Atlantique

– Lady Gaga made a surprise appearance at Coachella on Saturday night, announcing a new song just before the end of her 90-minute set, Us reports. "I've been so excited for this next part of the show because I've been trying to keep it a secret for so long," she told the crowd. "I've been in the studio and I'd like to debut a brand new song, 'The Cure.'" The singer later released "The Cure" on iTunes. "If I can't find the cure / I'll, I'll fix you with my love," she sings in the new song. "And if you say you're OK / I'm gonna heal you anyway / Promise I'll always be there / Promise I'll be the cure."

– "I am completely appalled at this decision," says the DA in Mesa County, Colo., where a convicted child molester was released from prison earlier this week. "I think the criminal justice system completely failed here." Michael McFadden, 46, was sentenced to more than 300 years in prison in 2015 after being convicted of sexually assaulting six young boys and girls, KKCO reports. He was released Tuesday after the Colorado Court of Appeals ruled that his right to a speedy trial had been violated. McFadden appealed, and the state Supreme Court decided not to hear the case last month. Now, McFadden is back behind bars. McFadden was convicted of 19 counts of sexual offenses in 2015, but his conviction was overturned last year after the court ruled that his right to a speedy trial had been violated because he had previously been convicted of sexual assault on a child in 1990. McFadden, who is not required to register as a sex offender, appealed, and the Colorado Court of Appeals ruled in his favor. The Mesa County DA appealed, but the state Supreme Court refused to hear the case. McFadden was arrested Friday on a warrant for failing to register as a sex offender, the AP reports.

But wasn't I a great candidate? Russia started their anti-US campaign in 2014, long before I announced that I would run for President. The results of the election were not impacted. They are spending too much time trying to prove Russian collusion with the Trump campaign - there is no collusion. Get back to the basics and make us all proud!" "We're losing sight of what we're going to do about the threat posed by Russians," former Director of National Intelligence James Clapper told CNN's State of the Union, per the Hill. "He never talks about that." Clapper, who oversaw the intelligence community's report that concluded Russia conducted an influence campaign in an

– Anderson Cooper is coming to daytime TV. The CNN anchor will host a one-hour daily show for national syndication in the fall of next year, according to a statement from the network. Cooper will continue to anchor his nightly AC360, Bloomberg reports. The daytime show will cover social issues, trends, events, pop culture, celebrities, and human interest news, according to the statement. “Over the course of the past few years, I’ve had the opportunity to work on a number of daytime programs,” Cooper said in the statement. “It’s fun and interesting to work in daytime television. The format is unique and you can really go in-depth on a wide range of fascinating and compelling stories. With this new program I hope to relay important information and relate to people and the audience in a completely different way.”

– Marco Rubio is pushing immigration reform hard today, telling Face the Nation that "we're not agreeing to a press release, we're agreeing to a piece of legislation, a bill." The Florida senator says that while "amnesty is the forgiveness of something," undocumented immigrants will face "consequences" if they violate the law. "If you're waiting to come legally to the United States now, no one who has done it the wrong way will get it before you," he said. "In fact, it will be much cheaper, faster, easier, and less bureaucratic if you're doing it the right way." Che Guevara was a racist that wrote extensively about the superiority of white Europeans over people of African descent, so he should inform himself on the guy that he’s propping up." Rubio on gun control: "Guns are what people are using. But violence is our problem." – If you’re looking for a new job, you might want to check out Glassdoor.com’s list of the most bizarre and difficult interview questions asked by top employers this year. Some of the questions, courtesy of job site Glassdoor.com: Goldman Sachs: “What is the average annual salary of a Goldman Sachs employee?” Google: “What is the average annual salary of a Google employee?” Facebook: “What is the average annual salary of a Facebook employee?” Click here for the complete list, or click here for 15 more weird interview questions.

– New Zealand Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern has become only the second elected leader in history to give birth while in office. The 37-year-old gave birth to a baby girl Thursday evening, four days after her due date, the BBC reports. "Welcome to our village wee one," Ardern wrote on Instagram alongside a photo of herself holding the child. "Feeling very lucky." She is the first elected leader to give birth since Pakistan's Benazir Bhutto in 1990, when she gave birth to a daughter while in office, the BBC reports. Ardern will be on maternity leave for six weeks. "I'm sure we're going through all of the emotions new parents go through, but at the same time feeling so grateful for all the kindness and best wishes from so many people," she said in a statement. "Thank you." She is the youngest prime minister the country has had since 1856. "I am not the first woman to work and have a baby; there are many women who have done this before," Ardern had said earlier this year. She is the first to take maternity leave while in office since Australian Prime Minister Tony Abbott did so in 2014. New Zealand's deputy prime minister Winston Peters will take Ardern's place in the running of the country

– A famous photo showing Union Gen. Ulysses S. Grant astride his horse at City Point, Virginia, during the Civil War is actually a composite of three separate images, the Library of Congress has revealed. While Photoshop made its debut in the 1980s, photographers in the 18th century had developed techniques to make photo montages, and the photo was actually created by LC Handy, a nephew of Civil War photographer Mathew Brady. When Brady died in 1896, Handy inherited a bunch of negatives from his studio and began "producing new prints from the negatives and licensing images to various publications," according to a press release. He also created "patriotic" images, "blurring the line between fact and fiction for those veterans who wanted mementos of their war experience, much like soldiers do today, in fact," says William B. Becker, creator of the online Trick Photography of Man with Two Heads. The photo showing Grant astride his horse, Cincinnati, at City Point was actually taken in 1864, but the horse in the picture has a "sock" around his left hind ankle, so he would have been riding Cincinnati at that time. Handy found a photo of Maj. Gen. Alexander McDowell McCook on horseback, facing left, taken in July 1864

– A 2,000-year-old Roman ship found off the coast of Liguria, Italy, was laden with garum—a condiment so popular at the time that Romans poured it on almost every dish, Discovery reports. The ship, which dates between the first and second century AD, was found at a depth of more than 650 feet, and archaeologists believe it was carrying between 2,000 and 3,000 clay jars filled with garum. "From the size of the jar-made mound we estimate the ship was carrying between 2,000 and 3,000 amphoras, or clay jars," team leader Simon Luca Trigona tells Discovery. The ship was likely sailing along the coast from Cadiz, Spain, to Rome, where garum was made, Discovery reports. The find was announced Thursday, and archaeologists say it's one of just five deep-sea Roman ships ever found in the Mediterranean. "No seaman would have drilled a hole in the keel, creating a potential way for water to enter the hull, unless there was a very powerful reason to do so," researchers write in the International Journal of Nautical Archaeology. They also found evidence of a fish tank on the ship, which could have held 440 pounds of live fish. (Click to read about another ancient Roman ship, this one much larger

– A new report from the US Energy Information Administration shows a milestone for renewable energy in the US: 10% of all electricity generated in the country in March came from wind and solar farms, the first time that's ever been recorded, Climate Central reports. April is also expected to see a record 10% of electricity generated by wind and solar farms, according to the EIA. Experts say the 10% mark shows renewable energy is becoming a major source of electricity in the US and can no longer be considered "alternative," though wind and solar will likely hit 15% by 2020, possibly sooner. March's 10% mark "says that the Trump Administration is living in the last century," says Jeremy Firestone of the Center for Carbon-free Power Integration at the University of Delaware. "Given price competitiveness of wind and commercial-scale solar and the public and corporate appetite for clean, renewable energy, an increasing fraction of US electricity will be generated renewably." Texas is the country's biggest wind power producer and California is the largest solar producer. Most states require a certain portion of their electricity to be generated from renewable energy, and some, such as California, are strengthening their goals. Infrastructure changes such as new transmission lines have also allowed developers to install large wind farms and connect them to population centers

– More than two dozen high school swimmers in San Diego had to be rescued Tuesday after going into the water against the advice of lifeguards, NBC San Diego reports. According to the Los Angeles Times, 64 high school swimmers and three coaches from Rancho Verde High School were in San Diego to compete in a long-distance swim. "We wouldn't advise a group this big to go in with the conditions we were experiencing," a San Diego Lifeguard Lt. John Sandmeyer tells NBC. "You can be the strongest swimmer in a pool and not be used to chop in your face, not to be used to the cold water when you go from 76-degree pool to 59-degree ocean." The students and coaches met with lifeguards before entering the water. The lifeguards recommended they postpone the swim, but the students were told they could handle it. "Hopefully it's a life lesson that helps them down the road," a San Diego Lifeguard Sgt. Ed Harris tells NBC. The group was about a half-mile from shore when panic set in. "It just snowballed into a mass rescue," Harris says. Lifeguards used jet skis, surfboards, and boats to rescue the students. One 16-year-old girl was unconscious and showing signs of hypothermia. She was

– A British woman's plea on crowdfunding site Go Fund Me to raise money for a homeless man's trip back to his native Jamaica has been a roaring success. Jenny Baker met the 64-year-old man, identified only as Michael, in Dalston on Saturday and offered to give him money, but when he asked for a ginger beer instead, she got him a sandwich and a drink, the BBC reports. "He told me that all he wants more than anything in the world is to go back to Jamaica where he says he will be fine and happy again," Baker wrote on Go Fund Me. "I have promised this man that this time next year he will be back in Jamaica and that I will make it my personal mission to make it happen. Anyway, let's #getmichaelhome spread the love, thank you xxx." Within 24 hours of her plea, more than $15,000 had been raised, and Baker says any additional funds raised will be used as a "start up fund" for Michael, the Independent reports.

– First daughter Ivanka Trump is defending a White House proposal to mandate paid leave for new parents in a letter to the editor published Wednesday in the Wall Street Journal, the AP reports. The paper had previously criticized the Trump administration plan "as bad policy and worse politics" that would increase taxes on business. The plan would be funded through unemployment insurance. But Ivanka Trump says in a response that: "Providing a national guaranteed paid-leave program—with a reasonable time limit and benefit cap—isn't an entitlement, it's an investment in America's working families." Ivanka Trump has met with Democratic and Republican lawmakers on the issue. The program has limited GOP backing, but more modest tax policies could be included in a Republican tax overhaul effort.

– Zack Brown's Kickstarter project—"I'm making potato salad. Basically I'm just making potato salad. I haven't decided what kind yet"—has raised more than $30,000 and is now 25 days away from its goal of $10. The simple project is one of many to make a splash on the crowd-funding site, which has loosened its rules to allow pretty much anything that isn't dangerous, regulated, or illegal to go on the site. "The world has changed in response to Kickstarter and we're changing with it," Kickstarter co-founder Charles Strickler tells Wired. "Hopefully that doesn't mean being overrun by knock-off Ray-Bans." For example, it's now OK to make misleading photo-realistic renderings of products like bath and beauty products, sunglasses, and Charity, genetically modified organisms, which were previously banned. But Brown's project isn't the only one making a splash on the site. A project called "Kickstarter Is Not a Store" has already raised more than $17,000 to create a USB charger for your phone, and a project called "Kickstarter Is For Fags" has already raised more than $3,300 to create a pirate ship.
